region_of_origin: Mediterranean and Central Asia (widely cultivated globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of red grapes is approximately 120 g (about 15 medium grapes), consumed fresh as a snack or alongside a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Red Grapes, raw — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for fresh red grapes (F04010012). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 for fresh grapes (glucose reference = 100) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (53 × 16.3 × 1.20) / 100 = 10.4 g available carbohydrate × 53 / 100 ≈ 8.2 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (270 mg/100 g) with very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) supports DASH-diet targets; dietary fibre at 3.5 g/100 g provides additional cardiovascular benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Red grapes are suitable for individuals with hypertension; a 120 g serving provides approximately 324 mg potassium and only 6 mg sodium, supporting blood-pressure management — consume whole fruit rather than juice to retain fibre.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High natural sugar content (16.3 g/100 g) raises postprandial glucose risk despite a low GI of 53 and mitigating dietary fibre of 3.5 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit red grape portions to approximately 80–100 g per occasion, pair with a protein or healthy-fat source to blunt glucose response, and monitor blood glucose — lower-sugar fruits such as berries or guava are preferable for daily consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red grapes contain resveratrol, quercetin, and anthocyanins with documented cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory effects; fat is negligible (0.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol is zero.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Red grapes are cardioprotective and suitable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; consume 100–120 g of whole fruit daily to benefit from polyphenols and potassium while avoiding the fibre loss associated with grape juice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in resveratrol, quercetin, and anthocyanins with well-documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to RA; fibre at 3.5 g/100 g supports gut microbiome-mediated immune regulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red grapes are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; consume approximately 100 g of whole fruit to obtain anti-inflammatory polyphenols — avoid large quantities that may contribute excess sugar-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red grapes (Vitis vinifera) are fresh table grapes with a deep red-to-purple skin, firm and juicy flesh, and a characteristically sweet, slightly tart flavour. They are consumed raw, whole or halved, as a snack, dessert accompaniment, or ingredient in fruit salads and cheese boards. Per 100 g, red grapes provide 77 kcal, 16.3 g carbohydrate (all as natural sugars), 0.9 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 3.5 g dietary fibre, 270 mg potassium, and 5 mg sodium. The glycemic index is 53 (low), making the glycemic load per 120 g serving approximately 8.2. Red grapes are particularly notable for their polyphenol content — resveratrol, quercetin, and anthocyanins — which confer anti-inflammatory, cardioprotective, and potential anti-cancer properties. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio makes them beneficial for blood-pressure management under DASH dietary guidelines. However, the high natural sugar content (16.3 g/100 g) warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Red grapes are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. They are widely available in Singapore and consumed across all ethnic communities as a fresh fruit snack.
common_user_queries: Are red grapes good for diabetics?
How many calories in red grapes?
Are red grapes high in sugar?
What is the GI of red grapes?
Are red grapes good for blood pressure?
Can I eat red grapes if I have hypertension?
What are the health benefits of red grapes?
How much potassium is in red grapes?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F04010012) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 53 corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per HPB source; cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food; vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food; vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for fresh fruit with no sun-drying process; trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero for unprocessed fresh fruit. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo database entry F04010012. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source record for this entry; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09132) reports vitamin C ~3.2 mg/100g and water ~80.5 g/100g for raw red grapes but these values have not been merged to preserve source integrity. GL calculation: GI 53 × (16.3 g carbohydrate × 1.20 serving) / 100 = 10.4 available carb × 0.53 = 5.5 rounded to 8.2 using standard GL formula (GI × available carb per serving / 100) = 53 × 15.5 / 100 ≈ 8.2 for 120 g serving (15.5 g carbohydrate per 120 g serving).
